definitions.definition: Lasting for a very short time; transitory.
definitions.example: The ephemeral beauty of cherry blossoms lasts only a week.

definitions.definition: An ephemeral plant or insect that has a very short life cycle.
definitions.example: Mayflies are ephemerals, living only a single day as adults.

etymology: From Greek ephemeros, from epi- (on) + hemera (day).

definitions.definition: The occurrence and development of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way.
definitions.example: A fortunate stroke of serendipity led him to discover the cure while working on something entirely different.

etymology: Coined by Horace Walpole in 1754, after the Persian fairy tale The Three Princes of Serendip.

definitions.example: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society, found in the hands of people of all ages.

etymology: From medieval Latin ubiquitas, from ubique (everywhere).

definitions.definition: A feeling of pensive sadness, typically with no obvious cause.
definitions.example: An air of melancholy hung over the abandoned town.

definitions.definition: Having a feeling of melancholy; sad and pensive.
definitions.example: He became melancholy at the thought of leaving his hometown.

etymology: From Greek melankholia, from melas (black) + khole (bile) — once thought to be caused by excess black bile.

definitions.definition: Able to withstand or recover quickly from difficult conditions.
definitions.example: Children are often more resilient than adults give them credit for.

etymology: From Latin resilire (to spring back), from re- (back) + salire (to jump).

definitions.definition: Resembling a labyrinth in complexity or irregularity; intricate and confusing.
definitions.example: The labyrinthine bureaucracy made it nearly impossible to obtain a simple permit.

etymology: From labyrinth, from Greek laburinthos — the maze of Crete built by Daedalus.

definitions.definition: Having mixed feelings or contradictory ideas about something or someone.
definitions.example: She felt ambivalent about leaving her job — excited for change but sad to leave her colleagues.

etymology: From Latin ambi- (both) + valentia (strength) — coined by Eugen Bleuler in 1910.

definitions.definition: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing; clearly expressing or indicating something.
definitions.example: Her eloquent speech moved the entire audience to tears.

etymology: From Latin eloquentem, from eloqui (to speak out), from ex- (out) + loqui (to speak).

definitions.definition: Tending to keep a firm hold of something; not readily relinquishing a position, principle, or course of action.
definitions.example: Despite repeated setbacks, she remained tenacious in her pursuit of justice.

etymology: From Latin tenax, from tenere (to hold).

definitions.definition: A sentimental longing or wistful affection for a period in the past.
definitions.example: The old photographs filled her with nostalgia for her childhood summers.

etymology: From Greek nostos (homecoming) + algos (pain) — originally a medical term for homesickness.
